sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 103, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Level 2 has a vertical divider, dark gray L-shape (left panel), yellow L-shape (left panel, below), black L-shape with white dots (right panel) - SPACE makes the yellow shape grow from 61 to 72 pixels (matching dark gray shape's size) and changes dot color from sky blue to white - After SPACE, DOWN now moves the dark gray shape by 3 rows - LEFT/RIGHT moves the dark gray shape by 6 cols, but the direction seems inconsistent - need to re-verify - Current: dark gray at rows 42-53, cols 21-35; yellow at rows 42-53, cols 3-17 - Goal: align dark gray shape with yellow shape (same position)
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 77 actions; a strong score needs about 50 or fewer. You are 1.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
RIGHT didn't work at rows 36-47, cols 0-14. So RIGHT is blocked at this position.
Let me summarize what I know about movement:
- UP: works at rows 36-47, moves to 33-44
- DOWN: works at rows 33-44, moves to 36-47; doesn't work at rows 36-47
- LEFT: works at rows 36-47, cols 6-20, moves to cols 0-14
- RIGHT: doesn't work at rows 36-47, cols 0-14; doesn't work at rows 33-44, cols 0-14
So RIGHT is blocked when the dark gray shape is at cols 0-14. This makes sense if there's a wall or boundary at the left edge.
Wait, but earlier RIGHT worked at rows 36-47, cols 6-20 to move to cols 12-26. So RIGHT works when the shape is not at the left edge.
Let me try RIGHT at rows 36-47, cols 6-20. From cols 0-14, I need to move RIGHT, but it's blocked. So I need to move to a different row position where RIGHT works.
Actually, let me reconsider. Earlier I observed:
- At rows 36-47, cols 12-26: LEFT moves to cols 6-20, RIGHT moves to cols 18-32 (or doesn't work)
- At rows 36-47, cols 6-20: LEFT moves to cols 0-14, RIGHT moves to cols 12-26
- At rows 36-47, cols 0-14: LEFT doesn't work (at left edge), RIGHT doesn't work
Wait, but I just tried RIGHT at rows 36-47, cols 0-14 and it didn't work. But earlier I tried LEFT at rows 36-47, cols 6-20 and it moved to cols 0-14. So the shape CAN be at cols 0-14, but RIGHT is blocked there.
This is strange. Let me check if RIGHT works at a different row position.
Actually, let me try a completely different approach. Let me move the dark gray shape to the TOP (rows 18-29) and see if RIGHT works there.
From rows 36-47, cols 0-14:
- UP: rows 33-44
- UP: rows 30-41
- UP: rows 27-38
- UP: rows 24-35
- UP: rows 21-32
- UP: rows 18-29
At rows 18-29, cols 0-14, let me try RIGHT.
But this would take 6 UP actions, which is a lot. Let me try a shorter sequence first.
Actually, let me try ACTION7 at rows 36-47, cols 0-14. ACTION7 moves the shape up by 3 rows.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Dark gray at rows 36-47, cols 0-14
# Try ACTION7 to move up
action(['ACTION7'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] >= 50:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Dark gray: row {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, col {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

# Try RIGHT after ACTION7
action(['RIGHT'])
seg2 = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg2:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] >= 50:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Dark gray after RIGHT: row {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, col {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

# Check for yellow dots
for n in seg2:
    if n['color'] == 'Y' and 1 < n['pixels'] < 50:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Yellow small: row {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, col {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Dark gray: row 33-44, col 0-14
Dark gray after RIGHT: row 33-44, col 0-14
Yellow small: row 57-63, col 63-63, pixels=7
Level completed: False
